summary:
The Reign of Terror (September 1793 – July 1794 AD) was the most violent phase of the [[event:107|French Revolution]], during which the radical Jacobin faction led by Maximilien Robespierre and the Committee of Public Safety executed approximately 17,000 people officially (with perhaps 40,000 dying in extrajudicial killings) in the name of revolutionary security, republican virtue, and emergency wartime governance. The Terror was triggered by multiple converging crises in 1793: counter-revolutionary insurrections in the Vendée (where a full-scale civil war raged), food shortages and sans-culotte radicalism in Paris, military defeats threatening France's borders, and the assassination of radical journalist Jean-Paul Marat by Charlotte Corday. The Convention responded by empowering the Committee of Public Safety and the Revolutionary Tribunal to try and execute enemies of the revolution with minimal procedural protection.

The guillotine — adopted as the Revolution's instrument of 'humane' capital punishment — became its most iconic symbol. The [[event:2481|execution of Louis XVI]] (January 1793) and subsequently Marie Antoinette (October 1793) were followed by waves of executions of nobles, clergy, federalists, and eventually rival revolutionaries — Girondins, then Dantonists (including Danton himself), then the Hébertists. By the spring of 1794, the Terror had reached a feverish intensity: the Law of 22 Prairial eliminated the right to legal counsel and required either acquittal or death, producing an accelerating conveyor belt of executions. Robespierre himself fell victim to the logic he had unleashed: on 9 Thermidor (July 27, 1794), the Convention turned against him, arrested him, and guillotined him the next day — ending the Terror through the same mechanism it had deployed. The Thermidorian Reaction that followed moved France toward the more moderate Directory, though the scars of the Terror permanently shaped how both conservatives and liberals thought about the dangers of radical democracy.
